speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is tape number three. We're interviewing Ernest Rubin, and it's March 19, 1995. We're in Brooklyn, New York. The interviewer is Rickey Halperin, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took this third Jew, his name was Klebner. I remember his name. And I remember the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came from where the Satmar Rabbi come from, Satmar. This used to be Romania-Hungary, you know? The same way like we were Czechoslovakia, and then Hungary, the same thing. This is Transylvania. You've probably heard, from Transylvania. So he said, you're going to be the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means the murderer. You go and finish him. So we were getting crazy. Everybody's frozen, you know? We-- we never saw things like this, you know? So he goes there, and he finishes this second Jew with a 2X4. He's jumping on his neck till his belly came out like this. And in-- in a minute or two, he was dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then everybody into bed. Go! Get lost! You know, they chased us. And we were happy that we don't see his fate. This guy, the stubenalteste was a German. Then I find out that he was there because he was a murderer. Because they had triangles. We had yellow triangle, I think down, if I remember well. Because we were Jews. They had triangles, I think, black up. That means they were murderers. But they got the positions because they were-- they knew how to take care of us. And they-- they put them in the concentration camps, in order-- that's the way they were punished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were-- they're getting positions in the barracks to take care of us. Stubenalteste, blockalteste, you know. So what should I tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I end up, like the other boys, they told me, that this is the Block 6, number 6, this is of worst. Constantly. You know, first of all, they put us into [NON-ENGLISH] [? kommando, ?] they called it. We went to Gusen. Sometimes we walked about four kilometers, five kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sometimes they took us by train. The-- the train was-- the wagons were half cut, you know? It was not covered. And when you were in the train, you were not supposed to stand. You had to keep your-- sit on the floor and keep your head between the legs in order you shouldn't see around, what's going on. So they put us into those tunnels, you know. I was-- I was a young boy. I never worked. Beating, and unbelievable, what was going on there. You know, they put me to the tunnel, tried to straighten out the concrete. You know, the way they filled up the concrete to make the tunnels.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we'd come home in the night, dead tired. You had to-- again, in the train. The train, the wagons were full of gravel, you know, small gravel. And you had to bend, go on your knees. That's the way you had to, all the way till you arrived to Gusen, to the camp. And you were not supposed to move. There was no space. In the knee, you know, the bone into the gravel, we were dying for pain. Because I was not supposed to get up and move. There was nowhere to move the gravel because all the floor was full of gravel. So that was going on for a few months. I forget to tell you, also, before we-- they started to take us to Gusen, you know, to-- in the tunnels, I was working in the [GERMAN]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stone quarries.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stone quarries, yeah. That was not so bad. We had to fill up those little metal wagons with the stones, and push it further.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's something there, the old prisoners told me, because we were hungry, yeah? We picked up some charcoal-- I don't know what it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was good to eat. I don't know if you know. And they explained it to me. They used to make margarine from-- from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- till today, I don't know what it was. I was eating it. And I-- it filled my stomach up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife, she went in the dictionary. And she said, yes, they were making margarine from it. So that-- I don't know how-- the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, that was for a few weeks, after they started to take us already, to-- to the tunnels there. So what shall I tell you there, what was going on? The beating. There was an SS. His arm was missing because he lost it on the Russian Front. What he was doing with us, the beating constantly, with the dogs, with the German shepherds, you know? And there was one Jew there, he couldn't take it anymore. So he goes, to the-- you know, you're not supposed to talk to the SS officer.

speaker: interviewee
text: He stands near me, this Jew, he goes there. He talks, Herr officers, you know, if you know what it means.

speaker: interviewer
text: Herr means mister officer, please, take my life. So he says, what you want-- what is your last wish? He said, my last wish? Give me a-- a-- a gallon of water to drink. He goes, he gives him the water. He drinking, you know, from the pail. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he says, you go out. Go up a little bit over the hill, where the SS is guarding the whole, you know, complex. All of a sudden I hear-- you are shot to death right away. Because we had to take him home, to the home-- I mean, back to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then from there, they took him to Gusen I, to the crematorium. Because the crematorium was in Gusen I. In-- in Gusen II, this was-- what shall I say? [GERMAN] in Germany, a destruction camp. You were not supposed to come out from there alive. This is just later, I found out. So what happened, after a few months, you know, of course, we start to see that this one is missing, the other one's missing. This one's died. The other one died. And the pleasure for the stubenalteste, the blockalteste, when we were home, instead of letting us rest, if somebody did any little thing, nothing, you know? We will go out for appell, yeah? And you didn't go fast enough, everybody is punished. How they going to punish us? Can I show it to you? Here is this picture. Where did I put it it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll-- we'll see it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where it is-- OK. What happened, what he did, he took a chair like this, yeah? Everybody had to bend over, hold the legs in the front. And this-- oh, did I lose something now?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. We have to sit while we're doing this. We have to sit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. With this kind, the reason I took this, because when I was driving the cab, just psychologically to have, I see what was going on with hold ups and everything, to have something in my hand. This is with what they used to hit us, 20-- sometime 25 on your behind, sometimes 20, sometimes 10, just depends what kind of mood he was. Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? One day, this-- doctors, they used to come. There was every barrack had the Jewish doctors doing-- they didn't give them better food, just look, in case some kind of a serious sickness comes out, you know? And you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was the other doctors who were not Jewish. They were-- they were first-aid place, emergency place in the Block 13. I was in Block 6. They were building, always, new blocks, new barracks, because they were bringing, always, new halfling in-- they called-- new prisoners. And this doctor, Doctor Thomas Martinez, he was a Spanish doctor. He came in, to the barrack. And I was on the chair. They were beating me with this on my behind, you know. After the second, third, you didn't feel anymore. You were-- you were numb. And I don't know what happened. He looks at me. And he had no right to say anything. Because this is the guy that's murdered the stubenalteste, he was doing this. This  was for pleasure. You know, when he punished one, it was-- or somebody did something, what he didn't like, everybody was punished. Everybody [? was ?] [? dead. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sometime he used to invite his friends, other-- from other barracks to give him the other, who can hit stronger? I saw one Russian, who was-- he was getting 25. And by the 15 hit, he dropped dead. So after this, he-- when he was-- I think I was getting 10. And he comes to me and he speaks to me in German. He says, listen, I'm going to take you out from here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Take you out. He said, don't say nothing. You're going to hear from me. He's also a prisoner. Next day, he come and I have a transfer. Me, another two boys. They need three boys to put into the emergency room there. When you come, somebody has a cut or you know, for the first aid, you need three boys to clean up the floor, the blood, the band aid.

speaker: interviewer
text: The band aid, it was paper. It was no-- it was no medical supply there. They took me out from there, imagine? And I had nothing to do anymore with Block 6. They put me into Block 13. Block 13, on this side of the end was the emergency room. Yeah? On this side of the-- the big barrack was a hospital. But what kind of hospital? You never get out from there. It was not-- once you get in there, you-- somebody couldn't walk anymore, they put them in there. No mattress, nothing, just on the wood, on the bunk. You know? They were just skin and bone, everybody. Nobody get out alive. So there were other boys there working in the-- in the kitchen, to wash the dishes there. And the first line, bed, you know, I was sleeping alone in the bunk beds, you know, three. So it was not like in Block 16-- Block 6. They're-- they're both, 6, 7, near each other. You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, I had, like, a little mattress. It was not so bad. It was not hard. And I had even one cover to cover myself. So for me, this was paradise. So-- and he says, when he-- listen, I-- you-- I going to save you, he says. You going to do-- you're going to do the work. And when the-- they bring the food for the doctors, they get some food from outside, somehow. They manage to get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all the food, they were getting heavier soup, full of potatoes. So what happened? They didn't eat though. So there was five doctors. Two Spaniards, one French, the second one was speaking French also. But he came from Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I ask him if he's Jewish. He said no. Because they wouldn't take a Hungarian non-Jew. I think he was afraid to say it. Because he had the better life than the other doctors in the barracks. And-- and a Russian, yeah. Yeah, and a Russian. I forgot his name. Tall guy. Here were five doctors. They were sleeping. That had nice room, you know, nice beds, all five of them, sleeping in one room. And when they brought the food for them, the suppliers, they brought in containers, like, maybe 20-gallon or something, or 15-gallon container. And they brought, maybe, so much soup for the five doctors and for the three of us, for eight people. So they gave us maybe three times so much food than the others. And the-- the food, because they needed the doctor, the one who supply the soup, they made it always like the potatoes should be in the bottom. This was paradise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened now? We're bringing the food in for them. They don't want to eat this. Because they managed to get salami, some stuff, you know, from outside somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said to us, you can do whatever you want. Because we couldn't eat so much. So what I-- I had three, four friends. The other two, they have their own, a few friends from the barrack. They knew that we have it better now. They all came. And we used-- they used to come with the plate and do it like this, you know, nobody should see it. Eat fast and run. So that's the way I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a cousin there, also a Rubin. I used to feed them. Until March, he was alive. I kept him alive. All of a sudden, I see they're bringing him in to Block 13, to the hospital there. I said, what's happened? He has-- you know, once you get the diarrhea, that was-- you knew already that a couple days or a week, you're going to die. So that's what happened with him. And I ask this Thomas, you know, the doctor, I say, help my cousin. I said, give him a-- he said, what can I give him? There is no medication. So they give us charcoal pills-- I don't know if you saw this-- [? they gave, ?] for diarrhea. But it was not enough. You needed the antibiotics, something. So all of a sudden, one of the boys are running over from the Block 13 here, where we were working. He said, Rubin, your cousin died. All of a sudden, I run. I see him already, outside, you know. Because it was like, wood in a forest. Maybe six, seven foot, dead bodies there, you know, outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought at least I'm going to bring him home. Because I felt that I'm going to make it. We didn't know when the war will be over. Just I thought, because you know, I have it a little bit better, I manage to bring him home, at least. He lived in the same town. My-- it's my grandfather's brother's grandson. Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after, he's dead. So they take him by-- you know, wagon. They are pushing it. They put, maybe, 10, 12 dead bodies. And the prisoners are pushing the wagon to Gusen I, that's where they were burning them. So that's the end of it. So what's happening after? End of March, start of April, I think, I get sick. I get diarrhea, excuse me. And fever, and heat. Thomas, he tries to help me. You know? He say-- because he told me, he said, Ernest, when-- after the liberation, I'm going to take you to Spain.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father is a big doctor in Madrid. I am a doctor. We're going to let you go to college and medical school. You will become a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: For me, it was fantasy I invent in here. And it went-- you know, because how? First of all, I didn't know then that he-- he is there because he was a communist. He was against Franco. You know the Revolution? The Civil War in, 19-- I think --36. That's the way he run away from Spain to France. And that's where the Germans arrested them as leftists. And they put them to Mauthausen. So what happened? He had a friend who was permitted to go out from the camp. Also a prisoner, he was-- he had the privilege to go to the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why, I don't know. He was a leader there working. He was the head of the working kommando. And he managed, through him, to bring two injections from the hospital, from the civilian hospital. I don't know what kind of injection. He gave me the two injections. And the two injections kept me alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I lost my appetite. But I was not able anymore-- I was getting weaker and weaker every day. What's happening all of a sudden, in April, in the middle of April, he's telling me, listen. They're taking all the Jews away. Where? I don't know. He didn't want to tell me or what. They're just taking us. So I had clothing, good clothing. Because he managed to get good clothing already, from the storage there, and good shoes. And what happened, when they started to go-- that was the death march already, going back to Mauthausen. We didn't know where, what. And the SS saw that they were a good pair of shoes. They took off the shoes. And they gave me a pair of sandals. Open, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing, what I had, I learned it when I was working in the tunnels there. They teach me where the cement bags, you know, where they had the cement. When the bags were empty, I used to take two, three, bags, take away the first lining, where the cement was. Because it was like, three-- three times double. And I cut out the head from the end and make shirts. And this was very warm. Because we were-- it was still snow there. And I managed to make underwear from it, under my pants. And the-- the paper is-- it keeps you warm. And for the sandals, I put paper and paper and paper. And then I put the socks above. But because I was wet in the sandal, the water was going in. The paper fell apart. So that's the way we-- they take us to Mauthausen. We get to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before we leave, would like to mention, again, the name of this doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Doctor Thomas Martinez.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you explain his kindness to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He saw me, that they are beating me. Something happened to him. Who knows? Maybe he thought maybe I could be his brother or something. I don't know. Because they-- they needed three boys then. And they-- they took a Russian and another guy who had here, a supermarket, by coincidence. I went in, Gorgette told you. And I see, I recognize him. I see his face. And him also, that's-- we, all three of us, survived like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many months did you work under him in that hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been the last six month. But the first six month for me was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you worked for the-- in the tunnels, do you remember the name of the company you worked for that-- that was building this? No. I know one thing. That when the tunnels were finished, there were chains, you know, bringing the material. They were making parts for the Messerschmitt planes. I think Messerschmitt. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Messerschmitt, yeah. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it-- was it that company, Messerschmitt, or [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I didn't know. Because then they-- they-- they kept us in the-- in the camp, already, for awhile. They didn't took us to work. So I don't know what happened. Maybe the Red Cross said maybe we are too young or something, I don't know. So they kept us in the camp to die. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the Red Cross in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no, no. This-- the Red Cross was a bluff, then, I think. I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no presence of Red Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, nothing, nothing, nothing. The only thing I remember before they took us for the death march, from Gusen back to Mauthausen, I know that every day they came to bomb Linz, you know, 11 o'clock, hundreds of planes, coming to bomb. You know, 11 o'clock in the morning, coming to bomb. This-- this was twice a day. But in the hundreds, of planes. And before they took us from Gusen to Mauthausen on the death march, the only think I saw, there were some planes coming very, very low on the camp, taking pictures. And we-- we assumed that this must be the Allies or the English, or the-- the American. And I saw they shot down a few planes. And the pilot jumped out. This I saw. And before the pilot fell on the floor by the parachute, the SS shoot him dead, before he-- he dropped on the floor. This, I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now you're leaving Gusen on the death march to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Mauthausen. To Mauthausen, there's no more barracks, no more bed. It was just a tent, a big, big tent. There is no place to sit, even, just ground, cold, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So this Thomas, he gave me, like, a bread like this, with me. And a piece of cheese like this, you know, to keep myself. But I couldn't eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was two boys there. I don't know how, they smelled it, that I have it in the shirt. And after two or three days, I managed to hold it. You know, I was eating a little bit just to keep myself alive. And they grabbed it. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And they said even, thank you in Yiddish, [YIDDISH], I don't know if you know what it means. So that was the end of my bread. That was the end of my cheese. Food, they gave you bread. They were giving you maybe, for 10 or 12 people, a little bread like this. Just a little, tiny slice. So you couldn't even-- it was not enough to make a bite. And then they gave you a little tea or you know, or-- or coffee, I don't know what it was. A little soup, empty. Then after a week or so or two weeks, they take us, again, for the death march. And I meet, all of a sudden, in Mauthausen, I meet two people from my hometown who are in the labor camp. And they-- before the war was over, they were concentrating everybody to this point, because that was the place where everybody was supposed to get shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we start to walk. We walk about 20 kilometers a day. I'm weak. So one thing I knew, when-- once in a while, they gave us 10 minutes to rest, yeah? And once I sit down in the ditch there, you know, everyone in the-- you know, outside form the road. Once I don't get up right away, they're going to shoot me. This I knew, because I saw what was going on. So two boys whom I was feeding-- I didn't even know-- they came over to me, yeah? And one says to the other, come. You remember he helped us? He gave us food. He said, listen, we're going to grab you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were in good health. Those two, they were in good health. You know, health. You know. And they grabbed me by the arm and I had to get up. They-- one-- both grabbed me like, we are walking like this in order the minute that the SS shouldn't see that I'm too weak. And they schlepped me like that. We are walking together like this for three or four days. We were sleeping on a mountain in the water, in the snow. It was still snow there, in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till we get-- in the middle of the road, they used to give us a little piece of bread, so much. And a little bit of empty soup. Nothing, nothing, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until we end up in Gunskirchen. In Gunskirchen we walk in, maybe a kilometer from the road, from the main road, into the forest. That's-- that's a few barracks there. There's no bed, nothing. Just sit, the same thing like in Mauthausen, in the tent camp there. On the floor, in the mud, there is no place even, to lay down. If-- you are lucky if you find enough place between the-- you know, between the halfling and between the-- the prisoners to sit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Food? Forget it. I couldn't go anymore out, anymore, even to get a little soup. I was already finished. The only time when they went out for food, I laid down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was waiting to die. I didn't-- Somehow I didn't care, I didn't know, even. So all of a sudden, some boys are telling me, saying, [? Herschel, ?] Rubin, the Red Cross is giving out some kind of a little nosh of rye.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know what kind of little cake, something. I don't know. So how shall I get there? There is an SS officer, a wild animal, yelling and hitting everybody. So I'm afraid to go there to ask, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because if you are under, I don't know, 17 or something, you were considered a youngster. You know, I was supposed to get a little something. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I get out. He was about, I don't know, 100 feet away from where I was staying. I go there. And I said, Herr officers-- I tell him in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: He should-- you know, I'm 16 years old. And he should give me, you know, the portion, what, from the-- whatever. He had the pistol in the hand. [SOBBING] He had this-- a leaded thing like this. And he gives me a hit over the head. I split my head open. And I fell into the mud. And now I'm trying to run away from his eyes. Because I know that he going to kill-- shoot me. Because he keeps the pistol. You know, he was shooting left and right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and I managed to crawl. I don't know, I had enough power to crawl back to the barrack, just to escape his eyes. Because the murderous eyes in him, you know? I was-- I was so frightened that he going to kill me. I managed to get back to the barrack. And-- and I collapsed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my head, bleeding all over. I have the mark right here, on the head. And that's where I stayed. Already I couldn't go. Then a few days later, maybe, two. later that-- this was already-- I don't know exactly the date. It was May 5th, I think. It was a Friday. My friend, who lives in-- in Toronto, he told me it was a Friday. I thought it was May 6. All of a sudden, the boys come in. Said, there is no guard. The SS disappeared. And everybody's going out from the barracks. They're walking, walking. There's no guard. There's no SS. And they go. You know, they see it's free. They go all the way to the road, about half a mile.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this-- and they're coming back. The American Army is there. You understand? So we are liberated. So the one who was in-- strong enough, in good health, they started to run away. And they were hungry. They were going to break in, to the German storage places, where they had the food, in the railroad, the wagons. They break open, and they started to eat like pigs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about thousands of thousands of them, they dropped dead the same moment. Because they were-- you couldn't eat. Your stomach was not ready to. My luck was that I was-- that I couldn't go. I was too weak.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and there was one guy I used to give food also. And he told me he was a Rabbi at home. He's a Rabbi. And he comes with about five pound of sugar. You know, he found. He went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to him, listen, put a little bit, sugar, in my mouth. Because I had fever. I was thirsty. You know, I didn't know what good. Just give a little nourishment. So he puts, like, for a little bird, you know, like this with the finger.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, listen, do you remember, I saved your life? I gave you the food. You are a part of my four, five, six boys whom I was giving food. He says-- so he gives me a little bit more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he said, listen, I cannot give you it. I need it for myself. And he disappeared. He went back because he knew, already, the road. And-- I stayed there, in the barrack there, on the floor. See, everybody left. The only people are hundreds of people dead around me, and a few people like me, who couldn't get up anymore. You know, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is the end of this tape. We'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
